title: Xi's Zero Tariff Offer to Africa and the Political Boundaries of Eswatini's Exclusion | African Arguments
gist: China's February 2026 zero-tariff offer to 53 African countries (effective 1 May) pointedly excludes Eswatini - Africa's last state recognising Taiwan - exposing that Beijing's 'no strings attached' aid narrative is conditional on the One China policy, while the African Union's silence reveals the limits of continental solidarity against a partner that is Africa's largest single trading partner.

insights.claim: China's zero-tariff offer to 53 African countries pointedly excluded only Eswatini, demonstrating that access to Beijing's economic perks is contingent on taking the politically correct side even within ostensibly universal programmes.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that Chinese trade access functions as a geopolitical instrument, relevant to assessing Taiwan-recognition risk premia.
insights.quote: getting economic perks from China can depend on whether you take the right political side, even in programs that are supposed to be open to everyone.

insights.claim: China announced in February 2026 that 53 African countries can sell goods to China tariff-free starting 1 May, framed by Xi as a response to global trade fragmentation.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: A concrete trade-policy shift expanding China-Africa commercial integration, a macro tailwind for compliant African exporters.

insights.claim: China's longstanding 'Five Nos' / non-interference promise (formalised 2018) of aid without political conditions is contradicted by the Eswatini exclusion, which proves political rules apply whenever Beijing's core sovereignty claims are involved.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Punctures the differentiator China uses against conditional Western aid, reframing how to read Chinese 'unconditional' offers.
insights.quote: this tax-free deal proves that political rules still matter deeply whenever the PRC’s core claims on sovereignty are involved.

insights.claim: The conditions on Chinese deals are not absent but narrowly focused on specific strategic goals - chiefly the One China policy - rather than the broad governance reforms Western aid requires.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: A sharper model for what Chinese conditionality actually targets, useful for forecasting which states face exclusion risk.
insights.quote: It’s not that there are no conditions, it is that the conditions are focused entirely on specific strategic goals.

insights.claim: Eswatini, a landlocked country of ~1.3 million people reliant on outside infrastructure funding, bears an economic rather than security cost from missing China's tariff deal due to its choice to keep ties with Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Quantifies the trade-off facing the last Taiwan-aligned African state, a case study in the price of diplomatic alignment.
insights.quote: This stance hurts Eswatini economically rather than threatening its safety.

insights.claim: Taiwan's President Lai Ching-te visited Eswatini in May 2026 after three pro-China African countries refused his plane airspace passage, and King Mswati III reaffirmed support for Taiwan's international recognition.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the active diplomatic contest and how Chinese influence constrains Taiwan's freedom of movement in Africa.

insights.claim: African governments and the AU stayed publicly silent on Eswatini's exclusion because China is the continent's biggest single trading partner and infrastructure financier, and because most states unaffected by recognising Beijing have little reason to protest.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Reveals the structural dependence that prevents collective African bargaining against China, a limit on continental agency.
insights.locator: A few major reasons explain this quiet reaction

insights.claim: China-Africa deals are negotiated mostly behind closed doors between top leaders, leaving little room for public debate on sensitive diplomatic decisions.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Opacity of China-Africa dealmaking is itself a structural feature limiting accountability and collective response.
insights.quote: China-Africa deals happen mostly behind closed doors between top leaders.

insights.claim: The case raises whether continental bodies like the AU can protect all member states when powerful external partners treat members differently, and whether African nations can collectively set the terms of major trade deals.
insights.speaker: Francis Annagu
insights.so_what: Flags the strategic question of African collective bargaining power versus great-power leverage.
